questions.prompt: Why does database-enforced authorization beat middleware-based?

choices.text: Defense in depth — even if a developer writes a query that forgot the user_id filter, RLS still applies. Single source of truth — the rule lives in one place (the policy) instead of duplicated across every endpoint. Auditable — all policies are queryable from pg_policies. Every consumer (auto-API, RPC, Edge Functions, direct SQL) respects the same rules. Middleware-based authz can be forgotten on a new endpoint, duplicated inconsistently, or bypassed by direct DB access. RLS makes authorization a property of the data, not the code path.

questions.explanation: Defense in depth + single source of truth + auditable. Database enforces.

questions.prompt: Enabled RLS on a new `posts` table. Queries return zero rows even for the table's owner. Why?

choices.text: RLS is enabled but no policies are defined. With RLS enabled and no policies, the table is locked — nothing is visible to non-superuser roles. You need to write at least one policy per action you want to allow (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE). Common pattern: 'Users can SELECT their own rows where author_id = auth.uid()' plus matching policies for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE with appropriate WITH CHECK clauses. RLS is opt-in security; you have to write the rules.

questions.explanation: RLS enabled with no policies = locked. Write at least one policy per action.
